title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Slow Miles: Life Aboard a Narrowboat
content.text: At a top speed of four miles per hour, a narrowboat is not a vessel that attracts people in a hurry. Yet Britain's canal network carries tens of thousands of residential boaters who have chosen to make these long, slender craft their permanent homes. For many of them, the decision was not about saving money — it was about reclaiming a different pace of life. (37) [37] Vivir en una barcaza es una elección de estilo de vida deliberada. [/37] 'I sold my flat in London and bought my boat on the same day,' says Clare Hobbs, who has lived on the canals for seven years. 'I have never once looked back.' || The narrowboat is a product of the Industrial Revolution. Britain's canal network was built in a frenzy of construction between roughly 1760 and 1850, designed to carry coal, pottery and raw materials from factories to cities and ports. (38) [38] Los canales fueron construidos para el transporte industrial. [/38] At their peak, the canals were the motorways of their age, carrying more freight than the roads and transforming the economic geography of Britain. The coming of the railways ended their commercial dominance within a generation. || Today, the network is maintained by the Canal and River Trust and used almost entirely for leisure and residential purposes. Restoration projects have reopened stretches of canal that had been abandoned and silted up for decades. (39) [39] La restauración de canales ha revitalizado comunidades rurales. [/39] Waterside towns that once turned their backs on derelict wharves have rediscovered their industrial heritage and invested in new moorings, cafés and visitor facilities. || Life aboard a narrowboat demands practical skills and a tolerance for limitation. A typical narrowboat is between six and seven feet wide and up to seventy feet long. (40) [40] El espacio interior es reducido pero puede ser sorprendentemente funcional. [/40] Living efficiently in a small space requires constant organisation, and systems for water, heating and waste must all be managed personally rather than delegated to a utility company. || The boating community is a close-knit one. Narrowboaters tend to help one another navigate difficult locks, share knowledge about the best moorings and keep a watchful eye on boats whose owners are away. (41) [41] El sentido de comunidad es uno de los aspectos más valorados por los residentes. [/41] 'People look out for each other on the water in a way you rarely experience in a city,' says Hobbs. || For many residential boaters, the appeal of the lifestyle ultimately comes down to something intangible. (42) [42] La vida en el canal ofrece una conexión especial con el entorno natural. [/42] Waking up beside a meadow one morning and a market town the next, watching herons fish from the towpath and kingfishers dart beneath bridges — these are pleasures, they say, that no address in any city can match.

paragraphs.A: It is a choice that prioritises experience over square footage and community over convenience.
paragraphs.B: Internally, clever design — folding furniture, concealed storage and compact kitchens — can make the space feel surprisingly generous.
paragraphs.C: Many converted wharves now house restaurants, galleries and weekend markets where factory floors once stood.
paragraphs.D: The goods moved by water included not just coal but textiles, grain and the fine ceramics of the Staffordshire potteries.
paragraphs.E: Few describe the canal as just a place to moor; most speak of it as a living, breathing community.
paragraphs.F: It connects them to the natural world in ways that a house on a street simply cannot.
paragraphs.G: Volunteering groups and charitable trusts have invested millions in dredging and rebuilding locks that had fallen into disrepair.

questions.explanation: A summarises the deliberate life choice before Clare Hobbs' quote about selling her London flat.
D expands on the goods transported by industrial canals, adding context before discussing their golden age.
G describes the volunteer and restoration projects that have reopened abandoned canals, supporting the revitalisation of communities.
B contrasts the limited space with the clever design that makes it functional, responding to the limitation mentioned before.
E generalises the communal spirit of the canal before Hobbs' quote about how they look after each other.
F introduces the connection with nature as an intangible element before the examples of the meadow, the herons and the kingfishers.
